questions.topic: Storage — disallow Shared Key authorization
questions.question: A security team wants to ensure that NO request to a storage account can succeed using the account key (Shared Key) — all access must use Microsoft Entra ID. Which configuration achieves this with the least operational complexity?

options.A: Rotate the storage account keys every 24 hours via Logic Apps.
options.B: Set 'Allow Shared Key access' to Disabled on the storage account so Shared Key authorization is rejected at the data plane, regardless of who possesses the key.
options.C: Set an Azure Policy that audits Shared Key requests in storage diagnostic logs.
options.D: Delete both storage account keys.

questions.rationale: Setting 'Allow Shared Key access' to Disabled on the storage account (control-plane property) causes the data plane to reject any Shared Key request and any service-or-account SAS (since those are signed with the account key). Only Microsoft Entra-authorized requests and user delegation SAS continue to work.

optionRationales.A: Rotation doesn't prevent use during the validity window.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Disabling Shared Key access is the supported, declarative control.
optionRationales.C: Auditing detects after the fact but does not prevent.
optionRationales.D: Deleting both keys breaks the account and blocks all rotation paths.

references.text: Prevent Shared Key authorization for an Azure Storage account

questions.topic: Storage — firewall, service endpoint, private endpoint
questions.question: A storage account holds data for an Azure Function in the same region. Security requires that traffic between the Function and the storage account never traverse the public internet, that no public endpoint exists, and that the account's public DNS name still resolves so existing on-prem clients (during migration) don't break. Which design meets all requirements?

options.A: Service endpoint on the Function's subnet + storage firewall allowing only that subnet, leaving public access on.
options.B: Disable public network access, add a private endpoint in the Function's subnet for the blob/file/queue services in use, and configure the corresponding Private DNS zones (privatelink.blob.core.windows.net, etc.) so the storage FQDN resolves to the private IP within the VNet.
options.C: Disable public network access and rely on Function's outbound IP allow-listing in the storage firewall.
options.D: Set the storage account to Premium SKU; private connectivity is automatic.

questions.rationale: Private endpoint + Private DNS removes public exposure (public access disabled) while preserving the public FQDN, which resolves to the private IP within the VNet via the Private DNS zone. Service endpoints leave the storage public endpoint reachable. Outbound IP allow-listing doesn't remove public exposure and is unreliable for Functions on Consumption.

optionRationales.A: Service endpoints don't remove public exposure.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Private endpoint + Private DNS is the documented zero-public-exposure pattern.
optionRationales.C: Public access disabled means even the function's outbound IP wouldn't reach the account without a private endpoint.
optionRationales.D: SKU choice doesn't enable private connectivity.

references.text: Use private endpoints for Azure Storage
Configure Azure Storage firewalls and virtual networks

questions.topic: Defender for Storage — malware scanning on upload
questions.question: A storage account receives files from external partners through a public upload form. The SecOps team must block malware before it can be downloaded by anyone else and prefers an agentless solution. Which Defender for Storage feature addresses this?

options.A: Defender for Storage classic plan with hash analysis only.
options.B: Defender for Storage (new plan) with malware scanning enabled at the storage account or subscription level — scans every uploaded blob with Microsoft Defender Antivirus, returns a verdict (Malicious / No threats found / Failed / Skipped) via blob index tags and Event Grid, and supports automated quarantine playbooks.
options.C: Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps file policies.
options.D: Manual scheduling of an Azure Function with a third-party AV signature library.

questions.rationale: Malware scanning is the dedicated Defender for Storage capability in the new plan. It scans uploaded blobs agentlessly with Microsoft Defender Antivirus, emits results via blob index tags + Event Grid, and integrates with quarantine automation. The classic plan only does hash-based analysis (not full content scanning).

optionRationales.A: Classic hash analysis is signature-only and limited.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Malware scanning in the new Defender for Storage plan is the documented, supported solution.
optionRationales.C: Defender for Cloud Apps targets SaaS, not Azure Storage uploads.
optionRationales.D: Building your own with Functions is operationally heavy and lacks Microsoft's threat intel.

references.text: Overview of malware scanning in Defender for Storage
Defender for Storage overview

questions.topic: Defender for Storage — sensitive data threat detection
questions.question: Defender for Storage in the new plan offers sensitive data threat detection. Which statement most accurately describes what this feature does and what it depends on?

options.A: It encrypts blobs at rest using customer-managed keys.
options.B: It uses the Sensitive Data Discovery engine (the same engine used by Defender CSPM) to identify storage containers with sensitive data (Purview SITs and labels), then prioritizes alerts by sensitivity — e.g., a public-exposure alert on a container with PII becomes higher severity than the same alert on an empty container.
options.C: It performs real-time DLP on every blob download.
options.D: It requires Microsoft Sentinel to function.

questions.rationale: Sensitive data threat detection is configurable in the new Defender for Storage plan. It leverages the agentless Sensitive Data Discovery engine (shared with Defender CSPM) integrated with Microsoft Purview SITs and classification labels. Alerts that involve resources holding sensitive data are prioritized accordingly.

optionRationales.A: Encryption at rest is unrelated; that's a Storage Service Encryption feature.
optionRationales.B: Correct. SDD + Purview integration is the documented mechanism.
optionRationales.C: Sensitive data threat detection enriches existing alerts; it is not a real-time DLP enforcement.
optionRationales.D: Sentinel is optional, not required.

references.text: Sensitive data threat detection in Defender for Storage

questions.topic: Storage — blob immutability with time-based retention
questions.question: A regulatory requirement obligates a company to retain transaction logs unmodifiable for seven years. After commitment, the policy must not be shortenable by any administrator. Which Azure Storage feature meets this?

options.A: Soft delete with the maximum retention.
options.B: Container-level time-based immutability policy with the policy 'locked' — once locked, the retention can only be extended, not shortened or removed; combined with versioning if the data is overwritten in place.
options.C: Premium block blob tier.
options.D: Storage account resource lock of type CanNotDelete.

questions.rationale: Time-based immutability for blob containers enforces WORM semantics. The policy can be in an 'unlocked' (test) state initially and then 'locked' — once locked, the retention period cannot be shortened or removed and the policy cannot be deleted. This is the documented WORM/compliance feature for blobs.

optionRationales.A: Soft delete prevents accidental deletion within a window but does not prevent modification or shortening.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Locked time-based retention policy provides WORM.
optionRationales.C: Storage tier affects performance/cost, not immutability.
optionRationales.D: Locks prevent control-plane operations on the resource, not data-plane modifications.

references.text: Immutable storage with time-based retention policies
Set and manage immutability policies

questions.topic: Storage — encryption with customer-managed keys
questions.question: A storage account must be encrypted with a key the customer controls; key rotation must be automatic with no downtime, and the key vault should be isolated from the storage account's subscription. Which configuration meets this?

options.A: Configure customer-managed key (CMK) encryption on the storage account, set the key reference to a versionless key URI in a Key Vault (in a separate subscription), and grant the storage account's user-assigned managed identity Key Vault Crypto Service Encryption User role. Auto-rotate the key via Key Vault rotation policy.
options.B: Use Microsoft-managed keys; rotation is automatic and free.
options.C: Store the CMK in the same subscription as the storage account; cross-subscription is not supported.
options.D: Manually rotate the key by editing the storage account's encryption settings every 90 days.

questions.rationale: Storage CMK supports a versionless key URI; when Key Vault's rotation policy rotates the key, the storage account automatically picks up the latest version without manual intervention. The user-assigned managed identity model allows the Key Vault to live in a different subscription. The 'Key Vault Crypto Service Encryption User' role is the least-privilege RBAC role for this scenario.

optionRationales.A: Correct. Versionless URI + UAMI + Key Vault rotation policy is the documented zero-downtime auto-rotation pattern.
optionRationales.B: Microsoft-managed keys don't give the customer control over rotation.
optionRationales.C: Cross-subscription Key Vault is supported.
optionRationales.D: Manual rotation is unnecessary and brittle when auto-rotation is supported.

references.text: Customer-managed keys for Azure Storage encryption
Configure customer-managed keys in the same or a different tenant

questions.topic: Azure SQL — TDE service-managed vs customer-managed key
questions.question: A company wants TDE on Azure SQL Database where (1) they can revoke key access to render the database inaccessible during an incident, (2) the key lifecycle (rotation, deletion) is fully controlled by the customer, and (3) no re-encryption of database files is needed when transitioning. Which configuration meets all requirements?

options.A: Service-managed TDE — Microsoft handles the key, no customer control.
options.B: Customer-managed TDE (BYOK) with the key in Azure Key Vault (or Managed HSM); switching from service-managed to CMK only re-encrypts the database encryption key (DEK), which is fast and online — no re-encryption of database files.
options.C: Always Encrypted with deterministic encryption.
options.D: Disable TDE and use cell-level encryption.

questions.rationale: TDE with CMK (BYOK) gives the customer full lifecycle control of the protector key. Switching from service-managed to customer-managed only re-encrypts the DEK (fast/online) — the database files themselves remain encrypted with the same DEK. Revoking key access in Key Vault renders the database inaccessible.

optionRationales.A: Service-managed TDE doesn't give customer key control.
optionRationales.B: Correct. CMK BYOK satisfies all three requirements.
optionRationales.C: Always Encrypted protects specific columns; it is not equivalent to TDE encryption-at-rest.
optionRationales.D: Disabling TDE removes encryption at rest entirely.

references.text: Azure SQL TDE with customer-managed key (BYOK)
TDE overview for SQL Database and SQL Managed Instance

questions.topic: Azure SQL — Always Encrypted vs TDE
questions.question: A regulated workload requires that the database administrator cannot see clear-text values of credit-card numbers stored in a specific column — the data must remain encrypted at all times in the database engine, even during query processing. Which feature satisfies this?

options.A: TDE with customer-managed keys.
options.B: Always Encrypted with secure enclaves (or deterministic / randomized encryption without enclaves for limited query patterns) — the column encryption key is held by the client driver, the engine never sees plaintext, and DBAs cannot decrypt the data.
options.C: Dynamic Data Masking on the column.
options.D: Row-level security (RLS) on the column.

questions.rationale: Always Encrypted is the only Azure SQL feature that separates data owners (who can decrypt) from data managers (who cannot). The column encryption key resides on the client; the engine processes only ciphertext. Secure enclaves extend this to allow richer queries (range, pattern matching) while preserving the trust boundary.

optionRationales.A: TDE only encrypts data at rest — fully decrypted in memory and visible to DBAs.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Always Encrypted is the documented separation-of-duties feature.
optionRationales.C: Dynamic Data Masking only obscures values in result sets for unprivileged users; data is stored in clear text.
optionRationales.D: RLS controls which rows a user can see, not encryption.

references.text: Always Encrypted in Azure SQL
Always Encrypted with secure enclaves

questions.topic: Azure SQL — Microsoft Entra-only authentication
questions.question: A SQL Database server must reject all SQL authentication and only accept Microsoft Entra ID identities (users, groups, service principals, managed identities). Which configuration is correct?

options.A: Set the server-level admin to a Microsoft Entra group, keep SQL authentication enabled as a fallback for break-glass.
options.B: Enable 'Microsoft Entra-only authentication' on the logical server (or managed instance); SQL authentication is disabled for new connections, even by the SQL administrator account, until the setting is reversed.
options.C: Remove the SQL admin login from master.
options.D: Apply an Azure Policy that denies CREATE LOGIN statements.

questions.rationale: Microsoft Entra-only authentication is a server-level setting that disables SQL authentication entirely for new connections. Optionally combine it with an Azure Policy (Deny or Audit effect) to enforce it across all SQL servers/managed instances in a subscription.

optionRationales.A: Keeping SQL auth enabled defeats the requirement.
optionRationales.B: Correct. The dedicated 'Entra-only authentication' setting is the supported mechanism.
optionRationales.C: Removing the SQL admin login doesn't disable SQL auth for other logins.
optionRationales.D: Policies don't run inside the SQL engine; CREATE LOGIN happens internally.

references.text: Microsoft Entra-only authentication with Azure SQL
Azure Policy for Microsoft Entra-only authentication

questions.topic: Azure SQL — auditing destinations
questions.question: A SOC team needs Azure SQL Database audit events to be queryable in Microsoft Sentinel and retained for 1 year. The storage account that will receive the audit events is configured for Microsoft Entra-only authentication. Which configuration is correct?

options.A: Send audit events to Event Hubs only; Sentinel doesn't support direct SQL audit ingestion.
options.B: Enable SQL auditing with destinations: Log Analytics workspace (used by Sentinel) and a storage account; for the storage account use managed identity authentication (system-assigned or user-assigned) since the storage account requires Microsoft Entra authentication. Set retention to 365 days.
options.C: Use storage account keys as the storage authentication type for SQL auditing.
options.D: Enable auditing only on the database, not the server — server-level auditing is deprecated.

questions.rationale: SQL auditing supports multiple simultaneous destinations: Storage account, Log Analytics workspace, and Event Hubs. When the storage account enforces Entra-only authentication, the SQL server's managed identity must be used (not storage access keys). Sentinel queries data already in its Log Analytics workspace.

optionRationales.A: Sentinel reads from Log Analytics; SQL audit can write directly to Log Analytics.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Multi-destination + managed identity for storage is the documented configuration.
optionRationales.C: Storage keys are not accepted when the storage account is Entra-only.
optionRationales.D: Server-level auditing is preferred for inheritance, not deprecated.

references.text: Set up auditing for Azure SQL Database
Auditing for Azure SQL Database — limitations

questions.topic: Defender for Databases — what's covered
questions.question: Which statement about Microsoft Defender for Databases is correct?

options.A: It is a single plan that covers Azure SQL, SQL on VMs, PostgreSQL/MySQL, and Cosmos DB at one price.
options.B: It is an umbrella covering four separately-priced plans: Defender for Azure SQL Databases, Defender for SQL Servers on Machines, Defender for Open-Source Relational Databases (PostgreSQL/MySQL/MariaDB on Azure and AWS RDS), and Defender for Azure Cosmos DB. Each can be enabled per subscription.
options.C: It requires Microsoft Sentinel.
options.D: It is enabled automatically by Defender CSPM.

questions.rationale: Defender for Databases is the umbrella term; it bundles four independent plans with separate pricing. Each plan can be enabled at the subscription level or on individual resources, and provides threat detection (anomalous activity, brute force, SQL injection) specific to that database type.

optionRationales.A: There is no single unified price; the four plans are billed independently.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Four plans, separately priced, enabled per subscription or per resource.
optionRationales.C: Sentinel integration is optional.
optionRationales.D: Defender CSPM is a separate plan focused on posture; it does not auto-enable database threat protection.

references.text: Overview of Microsoft Defender for Databases
Protect databases with Defender for Databases

questions.topic: Defender for SQL — vulnerability assessment baseline
questions.question: Defender for Azure SQL Databases includes vulnerability assessment (VA). After the first scan, dozens of findings appear that the team considers acceptable for this database (e.g., a specific user permission that is intentional). How should the team prevent these from re-appearing as findings without globally suppressing the check?

options.A: Disable Defender for SQL on the database.
options.B: Approve the current state as the new baseline for those specific rules on that database; subsequent scans compare against the baseline, and matching findings show as Pass.
options.C: Add a Defender for Cloud suppression rule that excludes the SQL database entirely.
options.D: Edit the VA rules XML to remove the checks.

questions.rationale: VA in Defender for Azure SQL Databases supports per-database baselines: marking the current state as the baseline for a specific rule causes future scans to compare against it. Acceptable deviations show as Pass while genuinely new deviations from the baseline are flagged.

optionRationales.A: Disabling Defender loses protection broadly.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Per-rule baseline is the documented mechanism.
optionRationales.C: Suppressing the whole database loses too much coverage.
optionRationales.D: VA rules cannot be edited as XML.

references.text: Vulnerability assessment for Azure SQL Database

questions.topic: Defender for SQL Servers on Machines — onboarding
questions.question: An on-premises SQL Server 2019 instance running on a Windows VM must receive Defender for SQL threat protection. The VM is already connected to Azure with Azure Arc. Which steps are required?

options.A: Install SQL Server on an Azure VM and migrate the database.
options.B: Enable Defender for SQL Servers on Machines on the Azure subscription containing the Arc-enabled VM; the Defender for SQL extension is deployed automatically via Azure Arc Machines, gathers SQL telemetry, and surfaces threat alerts and vulnerability assessment findings in Defender for Cloud.
options.C: Forward SQL audit logs to Microsoft Sentinel via syslog.
options.D: Install Microsoft Defender for Endpoint on the VM; Defender for SQL coverage is included.

questions.rationale: Defender for SQL Servers on Machines extends SQL threat protection (anomalous queries, SQL injection, brute force) and vulnerability assessment to non-Azure SQL Server instances via Azure Arc-enabled servers. Enabling the plan triggers automatic extension deployment.

optionRationales.A: Migration is not required.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Arc + Defender for SQL Servers on Machines is the documented onboarding path.
optionRationales.C: Sentinel forwarding doesn't provide Defender for SQL's analytics.
optionRationales.D: Defender for Endpoint provides separate EDR protection.

references.text: Defender for SQL Servers on Machines overview
Enable Defender for SQL Servers on Machines

questions.topic: Defender for Open-Source DBs — supported types
questions.question: An organization runs Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server and Azure Database for MySQL Flexible Server. Which Defender plan should be enabled, and what does it detect?

options.A: Defender for Azure SQL Databases — covers all Azure databases.
options.B: Defender for Open-Source Relational Databases — detects anomalous activity such as granular brute-force attempts, behavioral deviations in access patterns, and threat-intelligence-based detections for Azure Database for MySQL and PostgreSQL (and AWS RDS variants).
options.C: Defender for Cosmos DB — covers any non-SQL database.
options.D: Defender for Storage — databases are stored on Azure Storage so this is sufficient.

questions.rationale: Defender for Open-Source Relational Databases is the dedicated plan for Azure Database for MySQL and PostgreSQL (plus AWS RDS variants in preview/GA). It provides brute-force attack detection, behavioral baselines, and threat-intelligence-based alerts for these engines.

optionRationales.A: Defender for Azure SQL is only for Azure SQL Database/MI.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Defender for Open-Source Relational Databases is the right plan.
optionRationales.C: Defender for Cosmos DB is specific to Cosmos DB, not MySQL/PostgreSQL.
optionRationales.D: Defender for Storage protects storage accounts, not database engines.

references.text: Enable Defender for open-source relational databases on Azure
Defender for Databases overview

questions.topic: Defender for Cosmos DB — what it monitors
questions.question: Which event would Microsoft Defender for Azure Cosmos DB most likely raise as a security alert?

options.A: An anomalous extraction of a large volume of documents from a Cosmos DB account by a previously-unused identity, suggesting potential data exfiltration.
options.B: A failed ARM deployment to a Cosmos DB account.
options.C: An expired primary read/write key.
options.D: A request from a known Azure datacenter IP.

questions.rationale: Defender for Cosmos DB detects suspicious data-access patterns: data exfiltration anomalies, suspicious access from rare locations or IP reputations, and key-misuse patterns. ARM deployment failures, expired keys, and Azure-internal IPs are normal operational events.

optionRationales.A: Correct. Exfiltration-style anomalies are a core Defender for Cosmos DB detection.
optionRationales.B: Deployment failures are not security alerts.
optionRationales.C: Cosmos keys don't expire automatically.
optionRationales.D: Azure datacenter IPs are not suspicious.

references.text: Microsoft Defender for Azure Cosmos DB
Enable Defender for Azure Cosmos DB

questions.topic: Azure SQL — Dynamic Data Masking
questions.question: An application returns credit card numbers to a CRM. Service representatives must only see the last four digits, while a small group of compliance analysts must see the full value for audits. Database admins should bypass the masking. Which feature meets this?

options.A: Always Encrypted with deterministic encryption.
options.B: Dynamic Data Masking on the credit-card column with a partial mask exposing the last 4 digits, plus the compliance analysts and dbo group added to the masking exemption list.
options.C: Row-Level Security with security predicates.
options.D: Cell-level encryption.

questions.rationale: Dynamic Data Masking is presentation-layer masking — it changes what a query sees in the result set based on the principal. Admins (db_owner) bypass masking by default; analysts can be added to the exempted users list. Always Encrypted hides values from everyone including the engine, which is too strong for this dual-audience requirement.

optionRationales.A: Always Encrypted would prevent service reps from getting even the last 4 digits in plaintext.
optionRationales.B: Correct. DDM is the presentation-layer masking with exemption lists.
optionRationales.C: RLS filters rows, not column values.
optionRationales.D: Cell-level encryption requires application-side key management and doesn't address the dual audience cleanly.

references.text: Dynamic data masking — Azure SQL

questions.topic: Azure SQL — Microsoft Defender for SQL alert response (SQL injection)
questions.question: Defender for Azure SQL raises a 'Potential SQL injection' alert on a public-facing application. The alert details show vulnerable code constructed query strings with user input. What is the most appropriate immediate response and longer-term remediation?

options.A: Disable the alert and accept the risk because the database is restored from backup nightly.
options.B: Investigate the alert (which calling identity, target query, source IP); throttle or block the offending source if active; coordinate with the development team to refactor the affected code to use parameterized queries / stored procedures, and add web application firewall (WAF) rules in front of the app.
options.C: Restore the database from a backup.
options.D: Enable Always Encrypted on every column in the schema.

questions.rationale: SQL injection alerts indicate a real application vulnerability. The standard response is investigate scope and impact, block the active source if exploitation is occurring, then remediate by switching to parameterized queries and adding a WAF as defense-in-depth. Backups and Always Encrypted do not address the injection vector.

optionRationales.A: Ignoring is unsafe and not a response.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Investigate + active blocking + code fix + WAF is the documented full response.
optionRationales.C: Restore doesn't prevent re-exploitation.
optionRationales.D: Always Encrypted protects column values but doesn't fix the injection vector.

references.text: Defender for Azure SQL alerts reference
Defender for Azure SQL overview

questions.topic: Storage — Azure Files identity-based access (Kerberos)
questions.question: An Azure Files share replaces a legacy on-prem SMB file server. Existing Windows clients must authenticate using their domain identity (no separate share key) and existing NTFS-style permissions must still apply. Which authentication mode is most appropriate?

options.A: AD DS authentication for Azure Files (or Microsoft Entra Kerberos for hybrid identities) with on-premises ACLs preserved.
options.B: Storage account key only.
options.C: Shared access signatures for every user.
options.D: Public anonymous access with allow-listed IP ranges.

questions.rationale: Azure Files supports identity-based access over SMB using on-premises AD DS, Microsoft Entra Kerberos (for hybrid identities), or Microsoft Entra Domain Services. With AD DS or Entra Kerberos, share-level RBAC + Windows NTFS ACLs control access, so existing permissions can be preserved.

optionRationales.A: Correct. AD DS / Entra Kerberos with NTFS ACLs is the documented mode for this scenario.
optionRationales.B: Storage account key is a shared secret — not per-user.
optionRationales.C: SAS per user is operationally heavy and not SMB-compatible.
optionRationales.D: Public anonymous access exposes sensitive files.

references.text: Overview of identity-based authentication for Azure Files

questions.topic: Storage — anonymous blob access prevention
questions.question: A SecOps team wants to ensure that no container in any storage account in a subscription is configured to allow anonymous (unauthenticated) blob access — i.e., 'public access level' must be 'Private' on every container. Which combination of controls is most effective?

options.A: Trust users to use the portal correctly.
options.B: Set the storage account's 'Allow Blob anonymous access' property to false (disables anonymous access at the account level regardless of container settings), and assign the built-in Azure Policy 'Storage accounts should prevent blob anonymous access' to audit/deny across the subscription.
options.C: Set container ACLs to deny everyone after creation.
options.D: Enable Defender for Storage; it blocks anonymous access automatically.

questions.rationale: Disabling 'Allow Blob anonymous access' at the account level is an absolute control: container-level public access flags are ignored. Pair with the built-in Azure Policy that audits or denies storage accounts permitting anonymous access for full tenant coverage.

optionRationales.A: Manual hygiene fails at scale.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Account-level control + Azure Policy is the documented pattern.
optionRationales.C: Per-container fix doesn't prevent new accounts/containers from being misconfigured.
optionRationales.D: Defender for Storage alerts on suspicious public-exposure changes but does not block anonymous access.

references.text: Remediate anonymous public read access for blob data

questions.topic: NSGs and ASGs — micro-segmentation
questions.question: A three-tier app (web / app / db) is being deployed into a single VNet with three subnets. NSG rules must allow only the documented inter-tier flows (web→app on 443, app→db on 1433), and the rule definitions must remain stable as VMs scale in/out of each tier. Which design best fits?

options.A: Hard-code each VM's private IP into NSG rules and update the NSG when VMs scale.
options.B: Create one ASG per tier (asg-web, asg-app, asg-db), assign each VM's NIC to the appropriate ASG on creation, and write NSG rules referencing ASGs as source/destination (e.g., allow asg-app → asg-db on 1433).
options.C: Place each tier in its own VNet and disable peering.
options.D: Use service tags such as VirtualNetwork as both source and destination to allow only intra-VNet traffic.

questions.rationale: Application Security Groups let you express NSG rules in terms of application roles rather than IP addresses. VMs joining/leaving a tier simply update ASG membership; NSG rules stay stable. This is the documented pattern for scalable, role-based micro-segmentation within a VNet.

optionRationales.A: Hard-coded IPs don't scale.
optionRationales.B: Correct. ASGs are the documented mechanism for role-based segmentation.
optionRationales.C: Separate VNets without peering blocks all communication, breaking the app.
optionRationales.D: VirtualNetwork tag matches the entire VNet — no inter-tier isolation.

references.text: Application security groups overview
Network security groups overview

questions.topic: NSG default rules and service tags
questions.question: A new subnet's NSG has no custom rules. A VM in it can reach the internet but cannot be reached from an Azure Load Balancer health probe even though no explicit deny rules exist. What explains this and what is the correct fix?

options.A: Default outbound rules block Azure Load Balancer; add an explicit outbound allow.
options.B: The default inbound rule 'AllowAzureLoadBalancerInBound' allows probes from the AzureLoadBalancer service tag, but if a custom rule with higher priority denies it, the probe is blocked. Remove the conflicting rule, or add an explicit rule with higher priority allowing the AzureLoadBalancer service tag.
options.C: Load Balancer health probes require an NSG attached to the load balancer's frontend IP.
options.D: Probes are blocked when the VM uses a private IP; assign a public IP to the VM.

questions.rationale: NSGs have three default inbound rules: AllowVnetInBound, AllowAzureLoadBalancerInBound, and DenyAllInBound. Probes come from the AzureLoadBalancer service tag and are allowed by default — unless a custom rule with higher priority (lower number) denies them. Identifying and fixing the conflicting rule (or adding an explicit allow above it) is the fix.

optionRationales.A: Outbound default rules allow Internet — probes are inbound.
optionRationales.B: Correct. The AzureLoadBalancer service tag default allow is being overridden by a custom rule.
optionRationales.C: NSGs cannot be attached to LB frontend IPs.
optionRationales.D: Internal probes use a documented source IP range (168.63.129.16 / AzureLoadBalancer tag); public IP is unrelated.

references.text: Network security group default rules
Service tags overview

questions.topic: Network flow logs — VNet flow logs vs NSG flow logs
questions.question: An organization currently uses NSG flow logs. Microsoft has announced a migration path to a successor product that captures all flows for a virtual network (including subnets without an NSG) and integrates with Traffic Analytics. Which is the recommended successor and what is the migration guidance?

options.A: Continue using NSG flow logs — there is no successor.
options.B: Virtual Network flow logs — they capture flows at the VNet level (not dependent on NSG attachment), are recommended for new deployments, and Microsoft provides documented steps to migrate existing NSG flow log configurations.
options.C: Replace NSG flow logs with Azure Activity logs.
options.D: Use Azure Monitor diagnostic settings on the subnet — flow logs are deprecated.

questions.rationale: Virtual Network flow logs are the documented successor to NSG flow logs. They are enabled at the VNet level (not per-NSG), capture all flows in the VNet, integrate with Traffic Analytics, and have a documented migration path from NSG flow logs.

optionRationales.A: NSG flow logs have a documented successor.
optionRationales.B: Correct. VNet flow logs are the recommended successor.
optionRationales.C: Activity logs cover control-plane operations, not data-plane traffic.
optionRationales.D: Subnet diagnostic settings don't replace flow logs.

references.text: Virtual network flow logs overview
Migrate NSG flow logs to VNet flow logs

questions.topic: Azure Virtual Network Manager — security admin rules
questions.question: A network security admin needs to enforce a deny of inbound SSH/RDP from the Internet across all VNets in the tenant, overriding any future application-team NSG misconfigurations. Application teams should retain control over their own NSGs for less sensitive rules. Which AVNM capability addresses this?

options.A: Force application teams to remove NSGs from all subnets.

Dynamic membership via Azure Policy ensures new VNets are picked up automatically.

optionRationales.B: Correct. Security admin rules + dynamic network group is the documented pattern.

questions.topic: AVNM — security admin rules with SQL MI and Databricks

questions.topic: Virtual WAN — Routing Intent

questions.topic: Azure Firewall Premium — TLS inspection

questions.topic: Azure Firewall — DNS proxy and FQDN tag rules

questions.topic: Microsoft Entra Private Access — Quick Access vs per-app

questions.topic: Azure private endpoint vs Private Link service
questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes Azure Private Endpoints from Azure Private Link Services?

options.A: They are the same thing with different names.

options.C: Private Link Service is used for inbound only; Private Endpoint is used for outbound only.
options.D: Private Endpoints require ExpressRoute; Private Link Services do not.

Together they enable private connectivity across tenants/VNets without exposing public IPs.

optionRationales.A: They are distinct concepts.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Consumer vs provider distinction is the right framing.
optionRationales.C: Both serve bi-directional inbound from the consumer's perspective.
optionRationales.D: Neither requires ExpressRoute.

questions.topic: Azure DDoS Protection — Network vs IP Protection

questions.topic: ExpressRoute — private peering vs Microsoft peering
questions.question: A company runs an ExpressRoute circuit to access Azure resources privately. questions.topic: Azure Bastion — Standard SKU features

questions.topic: AVNM — connectivity configurations (hub-and-spoke + direct connectivity)
questions.question: A network architect uses AVNM hub-and-spoke connectivity configuration so spokes can transit through the hub firewall. options.C: Use Virtual WAN instead.
options.D: Use a User Defined Route from each spoke to the other's private IP.

questions.topic: Azure Front Door — Private Link to origin

questions.topic: Azure Firewall — forced tunneling
questions.question: A regulated workload requires that all outbound Internet traffic from an Azure Firewall be forced through an on-premises proxy/firewall for inspection, rather than egressing directly from Azure. Which configuration enables this?

options.A: There is no way to force Azure Firewall outbound traffic to on-premises.
options.B: Deploy Azure Firewall in forced tunneling mode (requires the firewall to be created with the Management subnet and the 'Forced Tunneling' feature enabled) and add a UDR on the AzureFirewallSubnet pointing 0.0.0.0/0 to the on-premises NVA next-hop; the firewall management plane uses the dedicated Management subnet to remain reachable.

questions.topic: Private endpoint — Private DNS zone integration
questions.question: After deploying a Private Endpoint for an Azure Storage Blob service, a VM in the connected VNet still resolves the storage account FQDN to the public IP. What is the most likely root cause and the fix?

options.B: The VNet is not linked to the privatelink.blob.core.windows.net Private DNS zone (or no A record exists). Link the Private DNS zone to the VNet (auto-registration off, A record pointing the storage account FQDN to the private endpoint's private IP — either by enabling private DNS zone integration when creating the endpoint, or by configuring the zone manually).
options.C: Storage account requires public access to be enabled for Private Endpoint to work.
options.D: The VM must be rebooted after Private Endpoint creation.

Either enable Private DNS zone integration during endpoint creation (auto-creates and links the appropriate privatelink.* zone) or configure the zone manually. Without the zone linked to the VNet, public DNS continues to return the public IP.

optionRationales.A: Private Endpoints rely on Private DNS to redirect the FQDN.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Private DNS zone linked to the VNet + correct A record is the documented configuration.
optionRationales.C: Public access disabled is fine; DNS resolution is independent.
optionRationales.D: VM reboot doesn't help if DNS isn't configured.

questions.topic: Azure Firewall Premium — IDPS
questions.question: Azure Firewall Premium IDPS detects a known exploit attempt against a backend VM. The team wants this specific class of traffic to be blocked, not just alerted on, but only for HTTPS (TLS-inspected) flows. Which configuration achieves this?

options.A: Enable IDPS in Alert mode globally; IDPS blocks automatically when TLS inspection is on.
options.B: Configure IDPS to Alert and deny mode globally; with TLS inspection enabled, IDPS can inspect decrypted HTTPS traffic and block signature matches. Tune by adding IDPS signature exclusions for false positives and IDPS bypass rules for specific source/destination/protocol combinations.
options.C: IDPS only supports Alert mode; blocking requires a Network Virtual Appliance.
options.D: Enable IDPS in deny-only mode; alerts are suppressed and only blocks logged.

optionRationales.A: Alert mode never blocks.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Alert and deny + TLS inspection + exclusions/bypass is the documented configuration.
optionRationales.C: IDPS supports Deny mode.
optionRationales.D: There is no deny-only mode that suppresses alerts.

questions.topic: Azure SQL — auditing tamper resistance
questions.question: A regulator demands that Azure SQL audit logs cannot be deleted or altered for 90 days. Audit destination is a storage account. Which combination addresses this?

options.C: Enable soft delete on the storage account.
options.D: Set the audit retention on the SQL server to 90 days; the storage account need not be immutable.

The 'None' option is not supported for SQL audit.

optionRationales.A: Redundancy doesn't prevent deletion or modification.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Immutable storage + 'protected append writes' enabled is the documented configuration.
optionRationales.C: Soft delete is not WORM; it merely delays delete.
optionRationales.D: SQL audit retention setting doesn't prevent admin from deleting blobs in storage.

questions.topic: Azure Database for PostgreSQL — network controls
questions.question: An Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server must be reachable only from a specific Azure VNet's subnet and never from the Internet. Which deployment option supports this most directly?

questions.topic: Storage — service tag with NSG vs Private Endpoint
questions.question: A team wants Azure VMs in one subnet to be the only Azure resources that can reach a specific storage account, but they also want to keep using the storage account's standard public FQDN with no DNS changes. Which combination of controls is appropriate?

options.B: Private Endpoint in the subnet + Private DNS zone — but this changes DNS resolution.
options.C: NSG on the subnet allowing outbound to the Storage service tag.

optionRationales.B: Private Endpoint changes DNS, which the scenario rules out.
optionRationales.C: NSG outbound to a service tag controls VM-side egress to all Storage IPs, not which storage accounts allow them in.
optionRationales.D: Connection Monitor measures connectivity; it doesn't enforce access.

Virtual network service endpoints

questions.topic: Azure SQL Managed Instance — connectivity types
questions.question: An Azure SQL Managed Instance must be reachable from on-premises over ExpressRoute but never from the public Internet. Which configuration is correct?

options.A: Set the connection type to 'Proxy' and assign a public endpoint.
options.B: Deploy SQL Managed Instance in a delegated subnet of a VNet connected via ExpressRoute private peering; do not configure a public endpoint on the managed instance; on-prem clients reach the MI's private IP over the ExpressRoute / VNet peering.
options.C: Enable the public endpoint on the managed instance and use NSGs to allow only the on-prem IP range.
options.D: Place the MI in a public subnet and apply a service endpoint policy.

questions.rationale: SQL Managed Instance is deployed in a delegated subnet of a VNet. By default the public endpoint is disabled — on-prem clients connect over ExpressRoute private peering (or S2S VPN) to the MI's private IP. Public endpoint is optional and disabled by default in this scenario.

optionRationales.A: Connection type 'Proxy' affects how clients route through the gateway, not whether MI is public.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Private MI deployment + ExpressRoute is the documented topology.
optionRationales.C: Enabling the public endpoint exposes MI to the internet — not what's required.
optionRationales.D: MI is always in a private/delegated subnet; there is no 'public subnet'.

references.text: SQL Managed Instance connectivity architecture

questions.topic: Azure Firewall — Threat Intelligence based filtering
questions.question: Azure Firewall's Threat Intelligence-based filtering must alert and deny traffic to/from known malicious IPs and domains. Which configuration is correct?

options.A: Threat Intelligence is automatically Alert-only and cannot be set to Deny.
options.B: Set Threat Intelligence mode in the firewall policy to 'Alert and deny'; configure allow-lists for known false positives (specific IPs/FQDNs that the org needs to reach despite TI flagging).
options.C: Threat Intelligence requires Defender for Cloud and is configured there.
options.D: Threat Intelligence is per-rule, not global; add 'TI' as an attribute on each network rule.

questions.rationale: Threat Intelligence-based filtering in Azure Firewall is configured at the firewall policy level with modes Off / Alert only / Alert and deny. Allow-lists handle known false positives by exempting specific IPs/FQDNs from TI evaluation.

optionRationales.A: Deny mode exists and is the recommended production configuration.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Alert and deny + allow-list is the documented configuration.
optionRationales.C: Threat Intelligence is a firewall capability, configured in the firewall policy.
optionRationales.D: Threat Intelligence is a global setting, not per-rule.

references.text: Azure Firewall threat intelligence-based filtering

questions.topic: Azure SQL — Conditional Access on Microsoft Entra authentication
questions.question: A SQL Database is configured for Microsoft Entra-only authentication. The DBA wants to require MFA + compliant device for all interactive logins to the database via SSMS or Azure Data Studio. Where is this control enforced?

options.A: Inside Azure SQL — assign the 'sqladmin' role with MFA required.
options.B: Conditional Access policy targeting the 'Azure SQL Database' enterprise app (well-known service principal); the policy applies when SSMS / Azure Data Studio obtains an Entra ID token to call SQL, including MFA and compliant-device grants.
options.C: Azure Policy denyAction effect on SQL connections.
options.D: TDE with CMK rotation requiring MFA.

questions.rationale: Conditional Access policies apply to Microsoft Entra token acquisition. Targeting the Azure SQL Database service principal lets you enforce MFA and compliant-device requirements on any client (SSMS, ADS, app) requesting a SQL token. This is the documented mechanism for MFA-gated SQL access.

optionRationales.A: Azure SQL doesn't have a 'sqladmin role with MFA' setting.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Conditional Access on the Azure SQL service principal is the mechanism.
optionRationales.C: Azure Policy doesn't gate token acquisition.
optionRationales.D: TDE encrypts data at rest; unrelated to login MFA.

references.text: Use Conditional Access with Azure SQL Database

questions.topic: Storage — SAS expiration policy
questions.question: Auditors discover that a storage account has issued SAS tokens valid for 10 years. The team wants to ensure all future SAS tokens generated from the account have a maximum recommended validity of 7 days, with warnings/log entries when a longer SAS is generated. Which configuration achieves this?

options.A: Disable SAS entirely on the storage account.
options.B: Configure a SAS expiration policy on the storage account specifying a 7-day recommended interval; SAS generation operations whose expiration exceeds the interval produce a warning and (when Azure Storage logging via Azure Monitor is enabled) emit a log entry to the storage logs.
options.C: Use Azure Policy to deny SAS generation.
options.D: Force user delegation SAS only; SAS expiration policies don't apply to user delegation SAS.

questions.rationale: SAS expiration policy is a per-account setting that specifies a recommended SAS validity interval. SAS generations that exceed the interval produce a warning (and a log entry when Azure Storage logging is on). The policy applies to service and account SAS; user delegation SAS use their own expiration limits.

optionRationales.A: Disabling SAS breaks all legitimate use cases.
optionRationales.B: Correct. SAS expiration policy + Storage logging is the documented mechanism.
optionRationales.C: Azure Policy doesn't see SAS generation, which is client-side.
optionRationales.D: User delegation SAS aren't affected by this policy and the policy is still valuable for service/account SAS.

references.text: Create an expiration policy for shared access signatures

questions.topic: Network Watcher — Connection Troubleshoot
questions.question: A user reports that a VM in a hub-and-spoke topology cannot reach an Azure SQL Database over port 1433. The SecOps team must determine whether the packet is being dropped by an NSG, AVNM admin rule, UDR, Azure Firewall, or DNS — and at which hop. Which Network Watcher feature gives this end-to-end diagnosis?

options.A: Connection Monitor — for continuous synthetic probes.
options.B: Connection Troubleshoot — on-demand reachability test from the source VM to a destination (IP/FQDN/port); returns next-hop, latency, and any blocking rule (NSG / route / firewall) along the path.
options.C: Topology — visualizes the network only.
options.D: Packet Capture — captures raw traffic for offline analysis.

questions.rationale: Connection Troubleshoot is the on-demand point-in-time reachability test. It identifies the blocking component (NSG, UDR, firewall) and hop on the path, plus latency. Connection Monitor is the continuous version. Both rely on Network Watcher.

optionRationales.A: Connection Monitor is for continuous baseline monitoring.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Connection Troubleshoot is the on-demand path analyzer.
optionRationales.C: Topology only visualizes the layout.
optionRationales.D: Packet capture is for raw traffic, not a guided path analysis.

references.text: Network Watcher Connection Troubleshoot overview

questions.topic: VM disk encryption — Encryption at host vs Azure Disk Encryption
questions.question: A security team must encrypt VM data — including OS, data, temp disks, and disk caches — end-to-end without using VM CPU for encryption, and with customer-managed keys. They are starting from scratch (no existing ADE deployment). Which option is the Microsoft-recommended path going forward?

options.A: Azure Disk Encryption (ADE) using BitLocker (Windows) or DM-Crypt (Linux).
options.B: Encryption at host with a Disk Encryption Set (DES) referencing a customer-managed key in Azure Key Vault — encrypts OS, data, temp disks, and caches, doesn't use VM CPU, and is Microsoft's recommended path; ADE is scheduled for retirement on September 15, 2028.
options.C: Storage Service Encryption only (the default).
options.D: Disable disk encryption and rely on Defender for Storage.

questions.rationale: Encryption at host is the documented forward-going recommendation. It encrypts OS, data, and temp disks and caches at the host level, doesn't consume VM CPU, and supports customer-managed keys via Disk Encryption Sets. ADE is scheduled for retirement on September 15, 2028; Microsoft directs new deployments to encryption at host.

optionRationales.A: ADE is retiring; new deployments should use encryption at host.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Encryption at host + CMK is the recommended path.
optionRationales.C: SSE alone doesn't cover temp disk or caches.
optionRationales.D: Defender for Storage doesn't encrypt VM disks.

references.text: Overview of managed disk encryption options
Server-side encryption of Azure Disk Storage

questions.topic: Confidential VMs — confidential OS disk encryption
questions.question: A workload handles regulated data and must run on hardware where memory contents are encrypted and isolated from the hypervisor and host OS, with a secure boot chain and a virtual TPM whose attestation can be verified at runtime. Which VM type is appropriate?

options.A: Generation 1 VM with TDE on Azure SQL.
options.B: Azure Confidential VMs (DCasv5 / ECasv5 series) with confidential OS disk encryption — memory is encrypted by AMD SEV-SNP, secure boot and vTPM are present, and attestation can be verified via Microsoft Azure Attestation.
options.C: Generation 2 VM with encryption at host.
options.D: Spot VM with low-priority pricing.

questions.rationale: Confidential VMs (DCasv5/ECasv5) use AMD SEV-SNP to encrypt VM memory and provide hardware-rooted attestation. Confidential OS disk encryption binds the disk keys to the VM's TPM. Microsoft Azure Attestation verifies the boot/runtime state. This is the documented path for hardware-isolated, attestable workloads.

optionRationales.A: TDE protects DB files at rest in storage, not VM memory or boot.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Confidential VMs with confidential OS disk encryption are the documented solution.
optionRationales.C: Gen 2 + encryption at host is a good baseline but doesn't provide memory encryption or attestation.
optionRationales.D: VM pricing tier is irrelevant to confidentiality.

references.text: Confidential VM overview (DCasv5/ECasv5)

questions.topic: Azure Bastion — Standard SKU and native client
questions.question: Operators need to SSH from their local terminal (via az network bastion ssh) into Azure VMs without public IPs, and to RDP via mstsc.exe with copy-paste enabled. Which Bastion configuration is required?

options.A: Bastion Basic SKU with portal-only access.
options.B: Bastion Standard SKU (or higher) with the native client support setting enabled; users connect via az network bastion ssh / rdp from their local terminal/mstsc.
options.C: Bastion Developer SKU on a per-VNet basis.
options.D: Use a jump box VM with a public IP instead.

questions.rationale: Bastion Basic only allows browser-based portal connections. Standard SKU adds native client support (Windows/macOS/Linux), IP-based connection, host scaling, custom port, and shareable links. The native client experience (az network bastion ssh and mstsc) is the documented Standard SKU feature.

optionRationales.A: Basic doesn't support native client.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Standard SKU + native client feature is the documented configuration.
optionRationales.C: Developer SKU is for single dev/test VMs without scale.
optionRationales.D: A jump box with a public IP defeats the security goal.

questions.topic: JIT VM Access — prerequisites
questions.question: A SecOps team plans to enable Just-in-Time (JIT) VM access on production Azure VMs. Which Defender for Cloud plan is required, and what is a prerequisite for the VM itself?

options.A: Defender for Cloud free tier; no other prerequisites.
options.B: Microsoft Defender for Servers Plan 2 enabled on the subscription; the VM must be deployed via Azure Resource Manager (not Classic) and have an NSG or be protected by Azure Firewall (excluding Firewall Manager).
options.C: Defender CSPM only; JIT is a CSPM feature.
options.D: Microsoft Defender for Endpoint license per VM and a public IP on the VM.

questions.rationale: JIT VM Access requires Defender for Servers Plan 2 on the subscription. Supported VMs are ARM-deployed (not Classic), protected by an NSG or by Azure Firewall (not Azure Firewall Manager-managed). On request, JIT temporarily opens the requested port for the requesting IP and time window.

optionRationales.A: JIT requires Defender for Servers Plan 2.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Plan 2 + ARM deployment + NSG/Firewall is the documented prerequisite set.
optionRationales.C: JIT is a Defender for Servers feature, not Defender CSPM.
optionRationales.D: MDE license is for EDR, separate from JIT. JIT works on VMs without public IPs (e.g., behind Bastion).

questions.topic: Defender for Servers — Plan 1 vs Plan 2
questions.question: A team enables Defender for Servers Plan 1 and is missing the following features they expected: agentless scanning, JIT VM access, File Integrity Monitoring, and OS configuration assessment against MCSB. What is the gap?

options.A: These features require enabling Defender for Endpoint separately.
options.B: Those features are Plan 2-only. Plan 1 provides Defender for Endpoint integration (EDR) and agent-based vulnerability assessment; Plan 2 adds agentless scanning, JIT VM access, FIM, OS configuration assessment, regulatory compliance, premium MDVM features, and free data ingestion benefits.
options.C: Those features are deprecated; use Defender CSPM.
options.D: Those features require Defender for Cloud Apps.

questions.rationale: Defender for Servers Plan 1 focuses on EDR (Defender for Endpoint integration) and agent-based vulnerability assessment. Plan 2 is the superset, adding agentless scanning, JIT, FIM, OS configuration assessment against the Microsoft Cloud Security Benchmark, regulatory compliance, premium MDVM features, and the free Log Analytics ingestion benefit for specific data types.

optionRationales.A: MDE is included in both plans; not the gap.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Plan 2 is required for the listed features.
optionRationales.C: These features are not deprecated; they are Plan 2.
optionRationales.D: Defender for Cloud Apps is a separate product.

questions.topic: Defender for Servers — agentless vulnerability scanning vs agent-based
questions.question: A team wants vulnerability scanning that doesn't require deploying or maintaining any agent on the VM, supports Azure / AWS EC2 / GCP instances, and works on machines without inbound network connectivity. Which capability fits, and what plan is required?

options.A: Agent-based scanning via Defender for Endpoint sensor; Plan 1 is sufficient.
options.B: Agentless vulnerability scanning powered by Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management; requires Defender for Servers Plan 2 (or Defender CSPM). Uses snapshot-based scanning of the disk via Azure backbone — no agent needed.
options.C: Qualys BYOL scanner; included with all plans.
options.D: Azure Update Manager; covers vulnerability assessment.

questions.rationale: Agentless vulnerability scanning takes disk snapshots and analyzes them out-of-band — no agent installation or network connectivity is required on the VM. It uses Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management for analysis. Available with Defender for Servers Plan 2 or Defender CSPM.

optionRationales.A: Defender for Endpoint sensor is an agent; the scenario excludes agents.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Agentless vulnerability scanning is the documented capability.
optionRationales.C: Qualys BYOL is supported but is not the answer for this scenario.
optionRationales.D: Azure Update Manager handles patching, not vulnerability assessment.

questions.topic: Azure Arc — onboard non-Azure servers to Defender
questions.question: A SOC must extend Defender for Servers protection to an on-premises Linux server and an AWS EC2 instance. Both should appear in Defender for Cloud with EDR, vulnerability scanning, and posture recommendations. What is the canonical path?

options.A: Install Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) on both machines directly.
options.B: Connect AWS via the AWS connector in Defender for Cloud; install Azure Connected Machine Agent on the on-prem Linux server to onboard it as an Azure Arc-enabled server; enable Defender for Servers on the relevant subscription; the Defender for Endpoint extension is auto-provisioned.
options.C: Migrate both machines to Azure VMs.
options.D: Use Microsoft Sentinel agents on both machines.

questions.rationale: Azure Arc-enabled servers brings non-Azure machines into Azure as first-class resources. For AWS, the AWS connector in Defender for Cloud automates onboarding (or Arc onboarding scripts). Once Arc-enabled, enabling Defender for Servers on the subscription auto-deploys the Defender for Endpoint extension and surfaces all Defender for Servers features.

optionRationales.A: MMA is deprecated; Azure Arc + Defender extensions is the current pattern.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Arc + Defender for Cloud connectors + Defender for Servers plan is the documented onboarding.
optionRationales.C: Migration isn't required.
optionRationales.D: Sentinel agents handle log collection, not Defender for Servers protection.

questions.topic: File Integrity Monitoring — Defender for Servers Plan 2
questions.question: After enabling Defender for Servers Plan 2, the team must monitor critical OS files and registry keys for tampering. Which mechanism does Microsoft now use for File Integrity Monitoring (FIM)?

options.A: FIM continues to use the Log Analytics agent (MMA).
options.B: FIM is enabled via the Defender for Endpoint sensor (already deployed when DfS Plan 1/2 is on); configure baselines and tracked paths in Defender for Cloud; MMA-based FIM is deprecated.
options.C: FIM requires a separate Sentinel solution.
options.D: FIM is no longer available.

questions.rationale: FIM has been migrated to use the Defender for Endpoint sensor (which DfS deploys automatically). The legacy MMA-based FIM is being deprecated. Baselines and monitored paths are configured in the Defender for Cloud portal.

optionRationales.A: MMA is deprecated.
optionRationales.B: Correct. FIM via the MDE sensor is the current model.
optionRationales.C: FIM is a Defender for Servers feature, not a separate Sentinel solution.
optionRationales.D: FIM is still available.

questions.topic: Azure Machine Configuration — guest assignments
questions.question: An auditor requires that all Azure VMs in scope must have a specific local password policy and BitLocker enabled, with continuous attestation that the configuration is in place. Which Azure capability achieves this with a deny effect option?

options.A: Azure Update Manager for security patches.
options.B: Azure Machine Configuration (formerly Guest Configuration) — assigns and audits in-guest configurations to VMs via Azure Policy, with built-in compute security baselines (MCSB) and the option to combine with Policy denyAction effects to block non-compliant configurations.
options.C: Azure Bastion — enforces compliance at access time.
options.D: Microsoft Defender for Endpoint baselines only.

questions.rationale: Azure Machine Configuration extends Azure Policy into the VM guest OS — it can audit (and optionally remediate, for supported guest configurations) in-guest settings like local accounts, registry values, BitLocker state, and compliance against the MCSB compute security baseline. Integrates with Defender for Servers Plan 2 OS configuration assessment.

optionRationales.A: Update Manager is for OS patching, not configuration.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Machine Configuration is the documented in-guest audit/remediation mechanism.
optionRationales.C: Bastion is for management plane access.
optionRationales.D: MDE baselines exist but the comprehensive in-guest configuration audit is via Machine Configuration.

references.text: Azure Machine Configuration overview

questions.topic: Defender for Containers — Defender sensor architecture
questions.question: An AKS cluster is enrolled in Defender for Containers. The team sees DaemonSet pods named 'microsoft-defender-collector-ds-*' running in the kube-system namespace and notices the cluster requires outbound HTTPS connectivity. What is the role of these pods?

options.A: They collect Kubernetes audit logs from the AKS control plane.
options.B: They are the Defender sensor — a lightweight eBPF-based DaemonSet on every node that collects runtime telemetry (process events, network, Kubernetes events) and sends it outbound to the Defender for Cloud backend for runtime threat detection.
options.C: They are the Azure Policy for Kubernetes (Gatekeeper) admission controller.
options.D: They are an ingress controller required by Defender.

questions.rationale: The Defender sensor is a DaemonSet that uses eBPF to collect runtime telemetry from each AKS node and publishes it (outbound HTTPS) to the Defender for Cloud backend. AKS control-plane audit logs are collected agentlessly via the AKS resource provider, separately. Azure Policy for Kubernetes is a different add-on (Gatekeeper).

optionRationales.A: Control-plane audit logs are collected by AKS natively, not by the sensor.
optionRationales.B: Correct. The Defender sensor is the runtime telemetry collector.
optionRationales.C: Azure Policy for Kubernetes is a separate add-on.
optionRationales.D: Defender doesn't deploy ingress controllers.

references.text: Defender for Containers architecture
Defender for Containers overview

questions.topic: Defender for Containers — runtime antimalware blocking
questions.question: An AKS cluster is enrolled in Defender for Containers and the team enables runtime antimalware in blocking mode. Which statement most accurately describes the prerequisites and behavior?

options.A: Antimalware is provided by Microsoft Defender for Endpoint installed inside containers.
options.B: Runtime antimalware blocking is an enhanced Defender sensor capability — requires the Defender sensor (Helm-provisioned latest GA version for AKS, or k8s-extension with antimalware collector enabled for Arc-enabled K8s) and Security Admin permissions to author/modify antimalware policies; can detect and optionally block malicious file execution in real time.
options.C: Antimalware blocking requires a Linux kernel module and root daemonset access to the host.
options.D: Antimalware is delivered as a sidecar injected by an admission webhook into every Pod.

questions.rationale: Runtime antimalware in Defender for Containers extends the Defender sensor with a malware scanning engine. AKS requires Helm provisioning of the sensor (latest GA version); Arc-enabled multicloud requires the k8s-extension with antimalwareCollector enabled. Security Admin permissions are needed to author/modify antimalware policies; Security Reader is needed to view them.

optionRationales.A: MDE is not deployed into containers.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Enhanced Defender sensor with antimalware capability is the documented architecture.
optionRationales.C: It uses eBPF, not a custom kernel module.
optionRationales.D: Defender does not inject sidecars.

references.text: Container runtime antimalware detection and blocking

questions.topic: Defender for Containers — Defender Security Gating (admission control)
questions.question: Defender for Containers offers a feature that evaluates incoming Kubernetes deployments against security policies before workloads are admitted to the cluster. Which feature is this, and what is the mechanism?

options.A: Azure Policy for Kubernetes alone, enforcing constraint templates.
options.B: Defender Security Gating — an admission control layer that evaluates Pod specs (e.g., images with critical vulnerabilities, deployments running as root) against security policies before workloads run. Enabled in the Containers plan settings.
options.C: Microsoft Sentinel automation playbooks triggered on Kubernetes audit events.
options.D: Azure Firewall in front of the AKS API server.

questions.rationale: Defender Security Gating is the admission control component of Defender for Containers — it evaluates incoming Pod specs against policies (such as 'block images with critical CVEs') and can block them at admission. It complements (and is distinct from) Azure Policy for Kubernetes / Gatekeeper.

optionRationales.A: Azure Policy / Gatekeeper enforces policy but Defender Security Gating is the Defender-specific feature that includes vulnerability and Defender-context-aware policies.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Defender Security Gating is the documented Defender for Containers feature.
optionRationales.C: Sentinel playbooks operate post-event, not at admission.
optionRationales.D: Azure Firewall doesn't perform Kubernetes admission control.

references.text: Enable Defender for Containers — Defender Security Gating

questions.topic: AKS — Workload Identity replaces AAD Pod Identity
questions.question: An AKS workload uses AAD Pod Identity (v1) to call Azure resources. Microsoft has announced the deprecation of Pod Identity v1. What is the recommended successor and key architectural change?

options.A: Stay on Pod Identity v1; deprecation has been canceled.
options.B: Migrate to Microsoft Entra Workload ID — an OIDC-based federation between Kubernetes service accounts and Azure managed identities; no in-cluster controller (NMI) required, and no secret in the cluster.
options.C: Use Kubernetes Secrets to mount service principal credentials.
options.D: Use Azure Key Vault FlexVolume only.

questions.rationale: Microsoft Entra Workload ID is the supported successor to AAD Pod Identity v1. Instead of the NMI DaemonSet intercepting IMDS requests, Workload ID uses OIDC trust between the Kubernetes service account and a federated identity credential on a user-assigned managed identity / app registration. Tokens are obtained via the standard MSAL flow; no secret is stored in the cluster.

optionRationales.A: Pod Identity v1 deprecation is real and Microsoft directs customers to Workload ID.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Workload ID + federated identity credential is the documented successor.
optionRationales.C: Storing service principal secrets reverses the intent.
optionRationales.D: Key Vault FlexVolume is a related but separate feature for secret mounting.

references.text: Microsoft Entra Workload ID with AKS overview
Migrate from pod-managed identity to Workload ID

questions.topic: ACR — quarantine pattern + vulnerability assessment
questions.question: An Azure Container Registry must accept image pushes from many development teams but must prevent images with known critical CVEs from being deployed by downstream AKS clusters. Which combination achieves this?

options.A: Disable image pushes from all but a small set of trusted accounts.
options.B: Enable ACR image quarantine (only images that pass a configured scan are promoted to the standard repository) and enable Defender for Containers registry vulnerability assessment; AKS clusters pull only from the post-quarantine repository. Combine with Defender Security Gating to block deployment of vulnerable images.
options.C: Use AKS Network Policies to block image pulls.
options.D: Set the ACR SKU to Basic; vulnerability scanning is included.

questions.rationale: ACR image quarantine + Defender for Containers vulnerability assessment is the canonical 'scan-before-deploy' pattern: pushes land in quarantine; only images that pass vulnerability scanning are promoted. Defender Security Gating at the cluster adds a runtime check at admission.

optionRationales.A: Restricting pushes doesn't address image content.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Quarantine + Defender vulnerability assessment + Security Gating is the documented multi-layered approach.
optionRationales.C: Network policies don't differentiate by image vulnerability state.
optionRationales.D: Vulnerability assessment is provided by Defender for Containers, not the ACR Basic SKU.

references.text: Azure Container Registry image quarantine
Defender for Containers — vulnerability assessment

questions.topic: App Service — minimum TLS version and TLS 1.0/1.1 retirement
questions.question: On May 31, 2027, App Service retires inbound TLS 1.0 and 1.1 for the App Service platform. Which is the correct preparation strategy?

options.A: Wait for the retirement; Microsoft will automatically upgrade clients.
options.B: Before the date: identify apps where minTlsVersion or scmMinTlsVersion is 1.0/1.1 (via Azure services retirement workbook or audit policy); use the Minimum TLS Version Checker detector on each app to see which clients still use TLS 1.0/1.1; remediate the clients; set minTlsVersion and scmMinTlsVersion to 1.2 (or 1.3) on every app and slot.
options.C: Set the minimum TLS version to 1.0 to maintain compatibility forever.
options.D: Disable SCM site to bypass the requirement.

questions.rationale: Microsoft's documented preparation: discover apps allowing TLS 1.0/1.1 (workbook + Azure Policy audit), use the Minimum TLS Version Checker detector to surface real client traffic, work with client owners to upgrade, then set both minTlsVersion and scmMinTlsVersion to 1.2+ on every app AND every deployment slot.

optionRationales.A: Microsoft will not upgrade clients on the customer's behalf.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Discover → measure → remediate → enforce is the documented sequence.
optionRationales.C: TLS 1.0 will be rejected at the App Service front door after the date, regardless of app config.
optionRationales.D: Disabling SCM breaks deployments and doesn't bypass the platform-level retirement.

references.text: Manage minimum TLS versions for App Service
TLS/SSL in Azure App Service

questions.topic: App Service — access restrictions vs Private Endpoint
questions.question: An App Service Web App must be reachable only from a specific corporate IP range AND from one VNet's subnet, with no public access from anywhere else. Which combination is most appropriate?

options.A: App Service access restrictions: configure an Allow rule for the corporate IP range AND a service-endpoint-backed Allow rule for the VNet subnet (or use Private Endpoint for the VNet path); set the default action to Deny. Optionally apply the same to the SCM site.
options.B: Use Azure Firewall in front of App Service.
options.C: Disable public network access only.
options.D: Apply NSGs to the App Service subnet.

questions.rationale: App Service Access Restrictions allow rules by IP CIDR, VNet/subnet (via service endpoints), or service tag, with a default deny action. For VNet traffic, an alternative is a Private Endpoint on the App Service. SCM site has separate access restriction rules. NSGs don't apply to App Service (a PaaS) directly.

optionRationales.A: Correct. Access Restrictions + service endpoint or Private Endpoint is the documented pattern.
optionRationales.B: Azure Firewall sits in front of resources in a VNet; App Service is a PaaS endpoint outside the VNet by default.
optionRationales.C: Disabling public access doesn't allow the IP-based allow scenario.
optionRationales.D: NSGs don't apply to the App Service plane.

references.text: App Service Access Restrictions overview
Use Private Endpoints for App Service

questions.topic: Azure Functions — authentication
questions.question: An HTTP-triggered Azure Function must require Microsoft Entra ID authentication before executing, returning 401 to unauthenticated callers and providing the user/principal claims to the function code. Which configuration meets this with the least code?

options.A: Implement OAuth token validation manually inside the function code.
options.B: Enable App Service Authentication (Easy Auth) on the Function App with Microsoft Entra as the identity provider; set 'Unauthenticated requests' to 'HTTP 401 Unauthorized'; the Function receives the validated principal in headers/ClaimsPrincipal.
options.C: Use API Management JWT validation; Functions does not support authentication.
options.D: Use a function-level key only.

questions.rationale: App Service Authentication ('Easy Auth') is built-in pre-execution authentication for Functions and Web Apps. With Microsoft Entra as the IdP and 'HTTP 401 Unauthorized' for unauthenticated requests, tokens are validated by the platform and claims are passed to the function. Function keys provide a separate (and weaker) shared-secret model.

optionRationales.A: Manual validation works but is exactly what Easy Auth replaces.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Easy Auth with Entra is the documented zero-code pattern.
optionRationales.C: APIM is a valid in-front-of-functions pattern but the question asks for the Function-level mechanism.

questions.topic: Azure API Management — backend protection
questions.question: A team uses Azure API Management to front a backend API. They want JWT bearer tokens to be validated (issuer, audience, signature, required scope) at the APIM gateway, with invalid tokens rejected before the request reaches the backend. Which APIM policy is appropriate?

options.A: ip-filter — restricts callers by source IP.
options.B: validate-jwt — validates the JWT signature against the issuer's JWKS, checks claims (issuer, audience, required-claims, scopes), and returns 401 on failure before invoking the backend.

It supports JWKS-based signature validation, issuer/audience checks, required-claims (e.g., scp/scope), and configurable failure responses. It runs in the inbound section before backend invocation.

optionRationales.A: IP filtering is unrelated to token validation.
optionRationales.B: Correct. validate-jwt is the documented policy.
optionRationales.C: Rate limiting is a quota policy.
optionRationales.D: set-backend-service handles routing only.

questions.topic: Azure Logic Apps — managed identity to call protected APIs

questions.topic: Azure Container Apps — network isolation and identity

optionRationales.B: External ingress + firewall filtering still exposes a public endpoint.

questions.topic: Entra Agent ID — Conditional Access for autonomous agents
questions.question: An autonomous agent runs on a schedule and authenticates to Microsoft Graph using its own agent identity (client credentials, no user context). The security team must (1) block agents flagged as high risk by Entra ID Protection, (2) allow only agents reviewed and approved by the security team to access tenant resources. Which Conditional Access configuration meets both?

options.A: Apply a single Conditional Access policy targeting All users with grant = Block on high risk.
options.B: Create two Conditional Access policies targeting Agents (Preview) under Assignments: (1) 'Block high-risk agent identities' Microsoft-managed policy keyed on Agent risk = High; (2) a custom policy that targets all agent identities and excludes approved agents — either by using custom security attributes (recommended) or the enhanced object picker — with grant = Block.
options.C: Use Azure Firewall to filter traffic from agent IPs.
options.D: Manually review every agent sign-in in the sign-in logs.

questions.rationale: Conditional Access now supports the 'Agents (Preview)' assignment target. The Microsoft-managed 'Block high-risk agent identities' template uses Agent risk condition. For the allow-list pattern, target all agent identities and exclude approved ones (by custom security attribute like AgentApprovalStatus = HR_Approved, or via the enhanced object picker). Policies are evaluated against the agent identity, not a user.

optionRationales.A: Policies for users don't apply to agent identities operating in client-credentials flow.
optionRationales.B: Correct. Two policies + agent-targeted Conditional Access is the documented pattern.
optionRationales.C: Azure Firewall doesn't differentiate agents from other principals.
optionRationales.D: Manual log review isn't a preventive control.
