questions.topic: AI Gateway in APIM — token rate limiting per consumer
questions.question: Multiple internal apps share a single Azure OpenAI deployment with a 30k TPM quota. Without controls, one team's runaway loop has been monopolizing all tokens, blocking other apps. The team wants a per-subscription-key TPM limit of 500 with pre-calculation of prompt tokens (so the call is rejected before reaching the backend if the limit would be exceeded). Which capability achieves this?

options.A: Application Insights with sampling rules.
options.B: Use Azure API Management as an AI gateway in front of the model deployment, and apply the llm-token-limit policy keyed on Subscription.Id, with tokens-per-minute=500 and estimate-prompt-tokens=true — prevents excess calls from ever reaching the backend.
options.C: Resize the Azure OpenAI quota to be per-app.
options.D: Use Azure Front Door with rate-limiting WAF custom rules counting requests.

questions.rationale: AI Gateway (APIM's policy set for LLM APIs) provides llm-token-limit specifically for per-counter-key token rate limiting on LLM APIs. With estimate-prompt-tokens=true, APIM pre-computes the prompt's token count and rejects the request locally if it would exceed the limit — saving the backend call. Counter key can be subscription, IP, or any policy expression.

optionRationales.A: App Insights is observability, not throttling.
optionRationales.B: Correct. llm-token-limit policy is the documented AI Gateway capability.
optionRationales.C: Azure OpenAI quota cannot be split per app on a single deployment.
optionRationales.D: WAF rate limiting counts requests, not tokens, and can't pre-compute prompt tokens.

questions.topic: AI Gateway — token quotas per Foundry project
questions.question: Microsoft Foundry Control Plane integrates with AI Gateway to enforce per-project token limits. Which two enforcement dimensions does this provide, and what HTTP response codes are returned when each is exceeded?

options.A: Only TPM rate limit; returns 503.
options.B: TPM rate limit (returns 429 Too Many Requests) and total token quota over a window (hourly/daily/weekly/monthly/yearly, returns 403 Forbidden) — both apply at the project scope.
options.C: Token count per request only; returns 400 Bad Request.
options.D: Bytes per second only; returns 429.

questions.rationale: Foundry Control Plane with AI Gateway enforces both a TPM rate limit (per-minute, returns 429) and a total token quota over a configurable period (hourly/daily/weekly/monthly/yearly, returns 403). Both apply at the project scope. Concurrent requests in flight can briefly exceed the configured limit until responses are processed.

optionRationales.A: Both dimensions exist; 503 is incorrect.
optionRationales.B: Correct. TPM (429) + quota window (403) is the documented enforcement model.
optionRationales.C: Per-request token limits aren't the same as TPM/quota.
optionRationales.D: AI Gateway enforces tokens, not bytes.

questions.question: Microsoft Foundry guardrails consist of controls applied at specific intervention points. Which set of intervention points is supported for AGENTS (vs models)?

questions.topic: Foundry Guardrails — severity levels
questions.question: When configuring a Foundry guardrail control for content risks (Hate, Sexual, Self-harm, Violence), which severity level is the MOST RESTRICTIVE (flags the broadest range of content)?

options.A: High — flags only the most severe content.
options.B: Low — flags content at low severity and above, making it the most restrictive setting.
options.C: Medium — flags content at medium severity and above.
options.D: Off — flags nothing.

questions.rationale: Severity levels are 'at and above': Low flags content at low severity or worse (most restrictive), Medium flags medium-and-above, High flags only the most severe content (least restrictive). Off disables detection for that risk (and is only available for approved customers).

optionRationales.C: Medium is between Low and High.
optionRationales.D: Off disables the control entirely.
